text: The video displays an anatomical diagram of the extrahepatic biliary system, highlighting the gallbladder, cystic duct, and common bile duct.
The port position that I use is what I call as reverse Aries position as per the constellation of the stars. And this is where we start.

The surgeon begins dissection at the peritoneum near the neck of the gallbladder, aiming to define Calot's triangle. The dissection focuses on identifying the junction of the cystic duct and gallbladder neck, as well as structures like the node of Luschka and the cystic artery. The surgeon clears fibro-fatty tissue around the cystic artery and Calot's triangle, dissecting the anterior and posterior peritoneal folds and making an incision in the posterior peritoneum.

The surgeon performs additional clearance at the Calot's triangle, noting the anterior placement of the cystic duct. Posterior dissection is emphasized to prepare a tunnel, with the cystic artery and lymph node of Loon clearly defined. Careful clearance around these structures is visible to prevent injury.

The assistant uses laparoscopic instruments to apply clips on the cystic artery and duct. The surgeon then divides these structures and proceeds to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully maintaining a close proximity to the gallbladder to avoid major blood vessels and ensure a clear surgical field.

depending on the length of the history either you would usually encounter these areolar tissue or maybe dense adhesions inflammation wise as such but we were lucky in this case minimal adhesions were encountered and I could get the plane very easily and very smoothly Injuring the gallbladder is fairly easy and we should make sure that we do not do that. It usually happens either in the end or in the start and at this stage I'll be extra careful to make sure it doesn't happen. So that's the conclusion of the surgery. Very neat and clean plane, no bile spill, no blood spill. Fairly simple and straightforward surgery and we finished in about thirty odd minutes. Thank you for watching.
